title: Central Banks Tighten Despite Iran Deal as Dollar Dominance Gets a Fresh Test
subhead: The US-Iran peace pact sent oil prices tumbling, but central banks from Tokyo to Frankfurt still hiked rates this week — and the Fed signaled more to come. Meanwhile, the deal's financial terms raise uncomfortable questions about who really benefits when sanctions lift.

sections.heading: Global Rate-Hike Wave Rolls On, Iran Deal or Not
sections.body:
The week saw a coordinated tightening push across major economies that refused to wait for the Iran deal to cool inflation. The Bank of Japan raised its benchmark rate to 1% on Tuesday — the highest since 1995 — and flagged further hikes ahead, with ex-BOJ policymaker Sayuri Shirai telling Reuters that two more increases by March are possible. Japan's core CPI held at 1.4% year-on-year in May, below the 2% target, but the BOJ warned that raw-material pass-through risks remain elevated. The yen, meanwhile, hovered near a 40-year low against the dollar, having barely budged despite the hike.

The ECB hiked rates last week and is keeping a July increase on the table. ECB Chief Economist Philip Lane said on Thursday the euro zone faces a 'mid-sized inflation shock' requiring a 'measured response,' while ECB Governing Council member Pierre Wunsch said a July hike remains in play if inflation evidence accumulates. The Iran deal has pulled oil off its peaks, but Lane stressed the pass-through to core prices is still unfolding.

The Fed held steady on Wednesday under new Chair Kevin Warsh's debut, but the meeting was anything but dovish. The updated dot plot flagged a possible rate hike by year-end, markets repriced accordingly, and UBS pushed its expected easing timeline to March 2027 — meaning no cuts this year. US inflation stands at a three-year high. Dow Jones reported that money markets now fully price a 25bp hike by October, a dramatic shift from March 2027 pricing just weeks ago.

Canada's inflation rose to 2.8% in April from 2.4%, and Indonesia has hiked three times in four weeks. The message is clear: the Iran deal eased the energy shock, but central banks are treating the inflation damage as structural, not transient.
sections.whyItMatters: The rate-hike cascade means the financial impact of the Iran war persists well beyond the ceasefire. Even as oil falls, central banks are tightening into the slowdown — a policy bet that inflation is sticky enough to warrant pain now.

sections.heading: Iran Deal's Financial Architecture: Oil Waivers, a $300B Fund, and the IRGC Paradox
sections.body:
The US-Iran memorandum of understanding signed around June 15-16 opens a 60-day negotiation window with immediate financial consequences. Under the draft, the US Treasury will issue waivers for Iranian crude oil, petrochemical, and derivative exports effective immediately. The US will lift its naval blockade, and both sides committed to restoring Strait of Hormuz traffic to pre-conflict levels within 30 days. Iran's frozen assets — estimated at around $100 billion — will be released, though no timeline was given.

The deal also references a $300 billion reconstruction fund financed by Gulf states, not US taxpayers. Trump publicly called reports of the fund 'ridiculous' and denied the US would invest in Iran, but Bloomberg's reporting indicates the fund language remains in the draft.

Perhaps the most consequential financial detail: sanctions relief will materially benefit Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, which controls sprawling commercial networks spanning oil, construction, shipping, and telecommunications. Reuters reported on June 20 that the IRGC — designated a terrorist organization by the US — is 'uniquely placed to capture a big chunk' of any financial windfall. Foreign firms returning to Iran would effectively operate alongside or through IRGC-linked entities, creating legal exposure under the US Justice Against Sponsors of Terrorism Act.

Simultaneously, the same administration cracked down on the crypto infrastructure Iran built to survive sanctions. On June 2, the Treasury sanctioned Nobitex and three other Iranian crypto exchanges for facilitating sanctions evasion using stablecoins, seizing between $450 million and $1 billion in Iranian-linked crypto assets. The dual message: Tehran gets dollar access back, but the crypto backdoor is closing.
sections.whyItMatters: The deal restores Iran to the dollar system as incentive for compliance — a deliberate strategy to reinforce dollar demand. But the IRGC's embedded economic power creates a fundamental tension: sanctions relief strengthens the very entity designated a terrorist organization.

sections.heading: Oil Prices Crash, Hormuz Recovery Will Take Months
sections.body:
Crude prices fell roughly 5% for a second consecutive session on Tuesday as the Iran deal's details emerged, touching a three-month low. But the selloff paused on Friday after the US and Iran called off the next phase of peace talks in Switzerland, introducing fresh uncertainty.

According to Reuters, banks estimate that a full recovery of oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz will take several months, even under the deal's 30-day target. Tankers remain partially trapped, and insurance and shipping logistics are still dislocated. The IEA's monthly report on Wednesday projected a significant oil supply surplus in 2027 once Hormuz flows normalize — but also confirmed that Russian crude output fell 10% below May targets, to 8.7 million bpd, due to Ukrainian drone strikes on energy infrastructure.

Separately, the IEA noted that Russian crude production dropped roughly 5% year-on-year in May, the first such decline attributed directly to Ukrainian attacks. This dual dynamic — a gradual Hormuz reopening and continued Russian supply disruption — is why analysts at The Economist argue 'oil prices will stay high for months' regardless of the deal.
sections.whyItMatters: Oil's crash on deal headlines masks a slower physical reality. The gap between financial-market pricing and actual barrel flows could persist for months, keeping energy costs elevated even as futures suggest relief.

sections.heading: G7 Critical Minerals Alliance Targets China's Rare-Earth Grip
sections.body:
The G7 summit in Evian produced a significant financial-geopolitics outcome beyond the Iran and Ukraine headlines: the launch of the G7 Critical Minerals Resilience and Production Alliance. The group set a target to reduce dependence on any single rare-earth supplier to below 60% by 2030, with a longer-term goal of 50%. China currently controls more than 60% of global rare-earth supply and most of the world's refining capacity.

The declaration covers 195 initiatives launched since early 2026, representing €64 billion ($73.5 billion) in investments, equity stakes, and offtake agreements. Key projects include Orion Critical Minerals' entry into Glencore's copper-cobalt assets in the DRC, Japan's expanded stake in Namibia's Lofdal rare-earth project, and Western-targeted development in Angola and Malawi.

Trump's proposed price-floor mechanism for critical minerals — reportedly backed by a Pentagon AI model — faced skepticism from G7 partners and industry, according to Reuters. The declaration lists it as an option under consideration rather than a commitment. Japan's PM Takaichi made economic security a centerpiece of her G7 debut, pushing the minerals agenda alongside broader supply-chain resilience.

The US also decided to hold off blacklisting China's DeepSeek and 100+ flagged firms to avoid escalating tensions, even with evidence that the AI firm distilled Anthropic's Claude model and supported Chinese military and intelligence operations. The geopolitical technology competition remains acute even when the diplomatic temperature is dialed down.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is building alternatives to Chinese mineral supply chains with real money and concrete targets — but the price-floor mechanism and DeepSeek restraint show the limits of Western coordination when economic costs and diplomatic risks collide.

sections.heading: Yen Near 40-Year Low, Japan Spends $72B Defending It
sections.body:
The Japanese yen is teetering near a 40-year low against the dollar, trading around 160 yen per dollar despite the BOJ's rate hike this week. The gap between US and Japanese interest rates continues to drive carry-trade outflows and inflate Japan's import bill.

PM Takaichi's government spent an estimated 11.7 trillion yen ($72 billion) on currency intervention last month, according to RTE. The scale of spending underscores the political salience of yen weakness in Japan — the currency's decline contributed to the political crisis that toppled PM Ishiba last year after rice price inflation devastated household budgets.

India's forex reserves dropped by nearly $10 billion to $671.6 billion in the week ended June 12, the steepest weekly decline since March, driven primarily by a fall in gold valuations as gold prices dropped 2.87% to $4,204/oz. The RBI has spent roughly $56 billion from reserves since the Iran conflict began in late February, intervening in both spot and forward markets to manage rupee volatility. India's reserves peaked at $728.5 billion on February 27 — just before the war started.
sections.whyItMatters: Both Japan and India are burning through reserves to defend their currencies. The Iran deal may ease energy import costs, but the interest-rate differentials driving currency pressure remain — and central banks can't intervene forever.

sections.heading: UK and Canada Hit Russia's Shadow Fleet and Energy Revenue at G7
sections.body:
The G7 produced a coordinated sanctions escalation targeting Russia's oil revenue channels. The UK sanctioned the banking arm of Yandex, two other lenders, and dozens of vessels accused of exporting Russian oil and gas — part of a broader package hitting Russia's 'shadow fleet' and a covert military procurement network. Canada simultaneously announced new sanctions on Russia's shadow fleet and energy revenues, and PM Carney raised China EV tariffs with Trump at the summit.

Zelenskiy told reporters at the G7 that leaders agreed 'Russia is not winning the war' and discussed additional sanctions to bring Moscow to negotiations. A joint leaders' statement appeared to bolster Kyiv's leverage in potential peace talks, and Trump signaled a swift return of sanctions on Russian oil.

Separately, South Korean President Lee Jae-myung told Trump at the G7 that sanctions on North Korea have become 'ineffective' due to military cooperation between Russia and Pyongyang — a candid acknowledgment from a US ally that the sanctions architecture is eroding.
sections.whyItMatters: The G7 is squeezing Russia's energy revenue harder, but the effectiveness question is real: if sanctions on North Korea failed due to Russian cooperation, Russia's own evasion networks may be equally resilient. The shadow fleet sanctions are a symptom of how financial warfare evolves — and how quickly it adapts.

sections.heading: Dollar Gets a Strategic Boost From the Iran Deal
sections.body:
The Iran deal's financial architecture is, in part, a dollar-infrastructure play. By restoring Iran's access to the dollar-denominated financial system — oil waivers, frozen asset releases through dollar channels, and a Gulf-funded reconstruction mechanism — the US is using dollar inclusion as incentive rather than dollar exclusion as punishment.

As CryptoBriefing noted, every barrel of Iranian oil priced in dollars reinforces dollar demand, and every frozen asset unfrozen through dollar channels reaffirms the system's centrality. The alternative — letting Iran permanently migrate to yuan-settled oil trades and crypto-based workarounds — would accelerate the fragmentation Washington is trying to prevent. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent's personal involvement signals the financial-architecture stakes.

The dollar index hit a one-year high on Friday, partly on rate-hike expectations and partly on the structural demand signal from the Iran deal. But the dollar's gain is uneven: gold has held gains on the Iran deal news, suggesting investors see both the de-escalation benefit and the long-term fragility of a system that can be weaponized and then un-weaponized at political whim.
sections.whyItMatters: The Iran deal inadvertently tests a proposition: can restoring dollar access reverse the dedollarization momentum that years of sanctions accelerated? The 60-day negotiation window will determine whether this is a strategic recalibration or a temporary pause.

bottomLine: The week's defining tension is between the Iran deal's immediate market relief and the enduring financial restructuring it can't undo. Oil fell 5%, but Hormuz won't fully reopen for months and Russian supply is down 10% from Ukrainian strikes. Central banks hiked anyway — the BOJ to a 31-year high, the ECB with another move possible in July, the Fed signaling a hike by October — because the inflation damage is done and sticky. The Iran deal's financial architecture is a dollar-infrastructure play that also enriches the IRGC, a paradox that will shape how sanctions relief actually works in practice. And the G7's new critical minerals alliance puts real money — €64 billion — behind supply-chain diversification from China, though disagreements on price floors and restraint on tech blacklists show the limits of coordination. The war's economic consequences are far from over; they've just moved from crisis mode to slow-burn mode.
sources: Reuters, Bloomberg, RTE, CryptoBriefing, Morning Star/Dow Jones, Business Standard, NDTV Profit, Japan Times, IEA, US News & World Report, Leadership Nigeria, Seeking Alpha, SCMP, Nikkei Asia, AP, The Economist, 247 Wall St
